Hello from Hatton! I'm here to help you with Blister Blight, a common disease affecting tea plants in our area.

**Symptoms:**

* Light brown depressions on leaves
* Blisters or swellings on lower leaf surface with white fuzz
* Leaf distortion, curling, and dead spots
* Young stems bend slightly

**Treatments:**

* Protective:
	+ Copper oxychloride (0.5-1% solution) to prevent the disease from spreading
	+ Bordeaux mixture (1:1:100) as a foliar spray every 7-10 days during rainy season
* Systemic:
	+ Hexaconazole (0.1-0.2%) or propiconazole (0.05%) for 2-3 applications to control the disease from within
* Cultural:
	+ Regulate shade trees to 50% cover to prevent moisture buildup
	+ Improve ventilation by removing weeds

**Practical Tips:**

* Keep your tea garden well-maintained and clean to prevent the spread of disease
* Monitor your plants regularly for any signs of Blister Blight
* Consider using Trichoderma harzianum, a natural biocontrol agent, as an alternative to chemicals

**Safety Note:** Always wear protective gear when handling chemical pesticides and fungicides. Make sure to follow the instructions on the label carefully and take necessary precautions to avoid exposure.

Hello there, fellow tea farmer from Hatton! I'm here to help you tackle the problem of Blister Blight on your tea plants.

**Symptoms:**

* Light brown depressions on leaves that get bigger and deeper
* White velvety spots on the lower leaf surface (that's where the blisters form)
* Leaf distortion, curling, or even necrosis (parts of the leaf die off)
* Some tender shoots may be affected by 10-30%
* Young stems might bend slightly

**Treatments:**

* **Protective measures:** Use Copper oxychloride (0.5-1% solution) or Bordeaux mixture (1:1:100) as foliar sprays every 7-10 days during the rainy season.
* **Systemic treatments:** Apply Hexaconazole (0.1-0.2%) or propiconazole (0.05%) for 2-3 applications.
* **Cultural practices:** Regulate shade trees to 50% cover and remove weeds to improve ventilation.

**Practical tips:**

* Keep your tea garden well-drained to prevent waterlogged soil, which can spread the disease.
* Prune infected shoots immediately to prevent further damage.

**Safety note:** Always wear protective clothing, gloves, and masks when handling chemicals or working with diseased plants.

Hello there, neighbor from Hatton! I'm here to help you understand Blister Blight, a common tea disease in our region.

**What does it look like?**

* Small, pinhole-sized spots on young leaves (less than 1 month old)
* Spots slightly enlarge but no blistering yet
* Mild on a few shoots
* Favored by cool and humid conditions

**How to treat it?**

* Improve air circulation through pruning and shade regulation (maintain 40-60% shade)
* Predictive spraying based on weather forecasts (e.g., if >80% RH for 3 days)
* No fungicide needed if caught early; monitor young shoots weekly
* Apply Trichoderma spp. or Bacillus-based bioagents as preventive foliar spray (1-2 g/L water, twice monthly)

**Practical tips from a local:**

* Prune your tea plants regularly to improve air circulation
* Use overhead irrigation sparingly to reduce humidity
* Monitor your young shoots weekly for any signs of the disease

Remember to handle fungicides and bioagents carefully, and follow all safety instructions when applying them. Wear protective gear, including gloves and a mask, to avoid exposure.

Hello there, neighbor! As a tea disease assistant from Hatton, I'm here to help you understand Blister Blight. This disease is quite common in our high-altitude tea gardens.

**Symptoms:**

• Severe blistering on the leaves and stems
• Brown, necrotic blisters that can kill the plant tissue
• Leaves will curl, distort, and drop off
• Stem distortion and dieback
• Widespread shoot dieback
• Secondary infections increase

**Treatments:**

• Urgent: Use systemic fungicides like tridemorph or contaf every 5-7 days for 2-3 weeks to avoid resistance.
• Heavy pruning of infected shoots (remove 15-20 cm below symptoms)
• Regulate nitrogen fertilizers to prevent succulent growth
• Consult experts for resistance checks and use bioagents consortia for long-term management

**Practical tips:**

* Keep a close eye on your tea gardens, especially during peak season.
* Use a combination of fungicides and cultural practices for effective management.
* Share knowledge with fellow farmers in Hatton to build a stronger tea community!

Remember to always wear protective gear when handling fungicides. Wash your hands thoroughly after use. Stay safe!
